subject: English Language Arts
topic: Plan, Build, Grow — Functional Workplace Communication

lesson_overview.overview: Students act as a junior school grounds team across 10 project weeks. They read a client request, compare design options, complete workplace forms, plant and monitor growth, and give a short oral client report. English outcomes focus on functional communication: reading practical texts, completing forms, using project vocabulary, and giving short oral updates. Weeks 4 and 8 are built-in flex weeks for pacing. Differentiation is embedded in all student-facing materials.
lesson_overview.essential_question: How can we communicate clearly while planning, building, and caring for a planter box for a real client?
lesson_overview.big_idea: Real work requires people to understand requests, communicate clearly, complete practical forms, follow steps, and report results.

sequence.focus: Meet the client. Identify the job. Use key project vocabulary.
Compare planter options. Choose a design. State a preference with a reason.
Complete a work order and build plan. Label materials and tools. Record dimensions.
Flex week. Build prep, ruler review, safety practice, or catch-up.
Build week. Follow steps, communicate with team, complete build checklist.
Planting day. Follow planting instructions, record what was planted, set up care schedule.
First growth observation. Measure, record, and complete a client update.
Flex week. Troubleshoot growth, review care routine, continue growth log.
Use data to make a recommendation. Graph results and draft a short explanation.
Present a short oral client report. Complete final recommendation card.

lesson_overview.differentiation_note: Three support levels are embedded in all student materials. Level A: pictures, circling, matching, dictated responses. Level B: word banks, sentence frames, partially completed tables. Level C: short written responses, open recording, simple explanations. Students may use different support levels for different tasks.
integrity_checks: Teacher-only guidance and conference notes remain in teacher-facing outputs.
Sentence frames and visual supports remain visible in student-facing materials.
Oral responses count as valid evidence throughout.
Flex weeks are intentionally open — use them to absorb delays or reteach.
Final client recommendation stays in the final response sheet on Week 10.

teacher_guide.big_idea: Functional English is real English. Students reading a client request, filling in a work order, and presenting a recommendation are doing grade-level communication work in a context that matters.
learning_goals: Understand a simple client request and identify key information.
Complete workplace-style forms using short practical language.
Use project vocabulary accurately in speech and writing.
Give short oral updates using sentence frames and cue cards.
Make a simple evidence-based recommendation to a real client.
materials: Oral Cue Card (Week 10 discussion prep sheet)
Final Recommendation Card (Week 10 final response sheet)
Sentence frame anchor chart posted in the room

timing.activity: Settle, visual agenda, and review last week's goal.
Direct instruction and teacher model using the week's target form or task.
Guided whole-group example — teacher completes one row or section together with students.
Supported student work time. Teacher circulates and conferences. Level A students may work with teacher or EA.
Oral share using sentence frame from the week.

teacher_notes: Model every form before students touch it. Students watch you complete one row first.
Keep only 2–3 visible design choices. Do not open up unlimited options.
Reuse the same sentence frames every week so they become automatic.
Oral responses count as valid evidence. Conference notes are assessment.
Weeks 4 and 8 are flex weeks — choose the activity that fits your class.
If build is delayed, run cardboard prototype activities instead.
If growth is slow on Week 7–8, focus on observation language and prediction rather than numbers.

week_8: Continue growth log when no growth has appeared yet
Begin graphing if enough data exists
Catch up any missing planting records

days.carryover_note: Completed Client Request Card carries forward to Week 2.

content.subtitle: This week: meet the client, understand the job, and get started.

content.scenario: A real client wants a planter box for the school grounds. Before we build anything, we need to understand exactly what they want.
prompts: Who is our client?
What do they want us to make?
Where will it go?

slides.title: How to read a client request

rows: Read or listen to the whole request before writing anything.
Find the three key things: who, what, and where.
Record the most important information on your Client Request Card.
client — the person or group who gives us the job
planter box — a container used to grow plants
dimensions — the size of something: length, width, and height
materials — what something is made from
budget — the amount of money available to spend

prompts: The client is ___.
The client needs a ___ .
We will put it ___ .

task_sheet.title: Week 1 Task Sheet — Client Request Card
instructions: Listen carefully while your teacher reads the client request.
Fill in each box using the information from the request.
Use the sentence frames to help you.

tasks.label: Part 1: The Client
tasks.prompt:
Fill in the Client Request Card.

The client is: ___________________
The job is: ___________________
The planter will go: ___________________
One important thing the client said: ___________________

render_hints.heading: Client Request Card
render_hints.help: Level A: circle the words your teacher wrote. Level B: use the word bank above. Level C: use your own words.

tasks.label: Part 2: What Is the Job?
tasks.prompt:
Answer these questions about the job.

What are we making? ___________________
Who is it for? ___________________
What does it need? ___________________
What will we do first? ___________________

render_hints.help: Sentence frame: We are making a ___ for ___ .

tasks.label: Part 3: Draw or label
tasks.prompt: Draw a simple planter box and label at least two parts. Use the vocabulary from the board to help you.

render_hints.help: Label ideas: soil, side, bottom, front, handle.

embedded_supports: Word bank: planter box, client, school, soil, seeds, wood, cardboard, measurements
Sentence frame: The client needs a ___ because ___ .
Level A: ask your teacher to scribe your answer, or circle from the choices on your support strip.
success_criteria: I can say who the client is.
I can explain what the job is.
I used at least two project vocabulary words.

exit_ticket.title: Week 1 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt: Complete this sentence: The client needs a ___ because ___ . We will put it ___ .
success_criteria: I named the client.
I described the job.
I used one project vocabulary word.

days.carryover_note: Bring Week 1 Client Request Card. Team choice carries forward to Week 3.

content.subtitle: This week: compare planter designs and choose the one that best fits the client's needs.

slides.title: What did we learn last week?

content.task: Use your Client Request Card to answer these quick review questions.
prompts: Who is the client?
What do they want built?
What is one important thing from the request?

rows: taller / shorter / same height
wider / narrower / same width
longer / shorter / same length
more expensive / less expensive / similar cost
easier to build / harder to build

content.task: Look at Planter A and Planter B. Use comparison words to describe how they are different.

prompts: We chose Planter ___ because ___ .
This planter fits the job because ___ .
One thing we are not sure about is ___ .

graphic_organizer.title: Planter Comparison Sheet
graphic_organizer.prompt: Compare the three planter options. Fill in what you notice about each one. Circle the one your team chooses at the bottom.
success_criteria: I compared at least two planters.
I recorded one number (size or cost) for each option.
I chose one planter and can explain why.

exit_ticket.title: Week 2 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt: Our team chose Planter ___ because ___ . One thing that fits the client's needs is ___ .
success_criteria: I named our planter choice.
I gave one reason for the choice.

days.day_label: Week 3: Work Order and Build Plan
days.carryover_note: Completed Work Order and Build Plan carry forward to Weeks 4 and 5.

slides.title: Week 3: Work Order and Build Plan

content.subtitle: This week: complete the plan so we are ready to build.

content.scenario: Last week we chose a planter design. Now we need to write a work order and a build plan before we can start building.
prompts: What planter did our team choose?
What does the client need the planter to do?
What information do we need in the plan?

slides.title: What goes in a work order?

steps: Team name and date
The client's name and the job
The item to build and where it will go
Materials and tools needed
What the team will do today

content.scenario: The build plan shows the size and shape of the planter before we cut or assemble anything.
prompts: Length: ___ cm
Width: ___ cm
Height: ___ cm
Number of main pieces: ___
Our plan is ___ (ready / almost ready / needs more work).
One thing I still need to add is ___ .
My teacher needs to check ___ .

task_sheet.title: Week 3 Task Sheet — Work Order and Build Plan
instructions: Complete the Work Order first, then move to the Build Plan.
Use your Team Choice Form from Week 2 to fill in the measurements.
Show your teacher when both forms are complete.

render_hints.heading: Work Order Form
render_hints.help: Level B: use the word bank. Level A: ask your teacher to help fill in the fields.

tasks.label: Build Plan — Dimensions
tasks.prompt:
Record the size of your planter.

Length: _______ cm     Width: _______ cm     Height: _______ cm
Number of pieces: _______

Draw the planter here and label the front, top, and one side.

render_hints.help: Sentence frame: Our planter is ___ cm long, ___ cm wide, and ___ cm high.

tasks.label: Materials List
tasks.prompt:
List the materials you need to build your planter. Use the picture bank on the wall to help.

1. ___________________
2. ___________________
3. ___________________
4. ___________________
5. ___________________

render_hints.help: Material ideas: wood boards, screws, soil, seeds, labels, cardboard, tape.

embedded_supports: Word bank: wood, cardboard, screws, tape, soil, seeds, ruler, saw, drill, hammer
Sentence frame: We need ___ pieces of ___ to build our planter.
Level A: use cut-and-paste material labels from the support strip.
success_criteria: My work order has a client name, job, and materials list.
My build plan shows three dimensions.
I labelled at least one part of my planter drawing.

checkpoint_sheet.title: Week 3 Teacher Checkpoint
checkpoint_sheet.checkpoint_focus: Verify work order and build plan completeness before build prep begins.
look_fors: Work order names the client, job, and materials.
Build plan shows length, width, and height.
Student can explain what each material is for.
Plan is feasible for the available build time.
conference_prompts: Walk me through your build plan. What happens first?
What materials do you need most?
Is anything in your plan not quite right yet?
checkpoint_sheet.release_rule: Release build prep activities only after Work Order and Build Plan are both complete and checked.

exit_ticket.title: Week 3 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt: Our planter is ___ cm long, ___ cm wide, and ___ cm high. We need ___ to build it.
success_criteria: I recorded three dimensions.
I named at least one material.

days.day_label: Week 4: Flex — Build Prep and Review
days.carryover_note: Flex week. Use to catch up, review, or prepare for building.

slides.title: Week 4: Build Prep and Flex Time

content.subtitle: This week: get ready to build. Your teacher will tell you which activity your class is doing today.

rows: Option A: Review ruler reading and check your plan dimensions.
Option B: Finish missing work orders or build plans from Week 3.
Option C: Make a cardboard prototype of your planter design.

content.task: Before we use any tools, go through this safety reminder together.
items: Listen for instructions before picking up any tool.
Keep tools on the table when not in use.
Ask before you cut or drill.
Tell the teacher if something does not feel safe.
Clean up your area before the end of the class.

content.scenario: Before we build, we need to make sure the plan is correct.
prompts: Do your three dimensions match your chosen planter?
Do you have all your materials on your list?
Is your team ready to start?
We are ___ (ready / almost ready / not ready yet) to build.
One thing we still need is ___ .
Our biggest question right now is ___ .

task_sheet.title: Week 4 Task Sheet — Build Prep
instructions: Complete the task your teacher assigns today.
If you finish early, move to the prototype sketch.
All forms must be finished before build week begins.

tasks.label: Build Prep Checklist
tasks.prompt:
Check each item. Put a checkmark when it is done.

☐ My Work Order is complete.
☐ My Build Plan has three dimensions.
☐ I know what materials I need.
☐ I know what my team role is.
☐ I reviewed the safety rules.
☐ I know what to do first on build day.
☐ My teacher has checked my plan.

tasks.label: Prototype Sketch
tasks.prompt: Draw a simple sketch of your planter as if you are looking at it from the front. Label the length, width, and height. Add any notes about how it will be put together.

render_hints.help: This is a planning sketch — it does not need to be perfect. Just show the main shape and measurements.

embedded_supports: Ask your teacher if you need to redo Week 2 or Week 3 forms.
Level A: your teacher will check boxes with you.
Sentence frame: We are ready because ___ .
success_criteria: My build prep checklist shows that I am ready.
I have all required forms complete.
I know my role for build week.

days.carryover_note: Build Checklist and teacher observation notes carry forward to Week 6.

content.subtitle: Today we build. Follow the steps, work as a team, and check your work against the plan.

steps: Listen to the safety review before touching any materials.
Check your role card and know your job for today.
Follow the build steps in order — do not skip ahead.
Call for your teacher before cutting, drilling, or fixing a mistake.
Check your measurements before each step.

content.scenario: Halfway through the build, stop and check your progress.
prompts: Is the size matching the plan?
Are all team members doing their role?
What do we still need to finish?

rows: Lay out all materials and count them before starting.
Measure and mark before you cut or attach anything.
Check the finished piece against the build plan before moving on.

prompts: Today we finished ___ .
Our planter is now ___ (complete / almost done / still needs work).
Next we need to ___ .

task_sheet.title: Week 5 Task Sheet — Build Checklist and Team Roles
instructions: Record your team role at the top.
Complete the Build Checklist as your team finishes each step.
Fill in the Oral Update Card at the end of the session.

tasks.label: Team Role
tasks.prompt:
My role today is: ___________________
My job description: ___________________
I am responsible for: ___________________

render_hints.heading: Group Role Card
render_hints.help: Role options: Measurer, Recorder, Materials Helper, Water Checker, Speaker/Reporter, Cleanup Lead.

tasks.label: Build Checklist
tasks.prompt:
Put a checkmark as your team completes each step.

☐ We looked at the plan before starting.
☐ We got our materials.
☐ We measured before cutting or attaching.
☐ We checked the size against the plan.
☐ We put our name on the project.
☐ We cleaned up our area.
☐ We did a final check.

tasks.label: Oral Update Card
tasks.prompt:
Prepare your oral update. Use these sentence frames.

Today we finished ___ .
Our team worked on ___ .
The hardest part was ___ .
Next we need to ___ .

render_hints.help: Practice saying your update out loud before writing it down.

embedded_supports: Level A: your teacher will circulate and ask you questions — speak your answers.
Level B: use the sentence frames above to write your update.
Level C: write a short paragraph describing what your team built and what comes next.
success_criteria: I completed my team role.
I checked off each step of the Build Checklist.
I can give a short oral update about what we did.

checkpoint_sheet.title: Week 5 Teacher Observation — Build Day
checkpoint_sheet.checkpoint_focus: Observe participation, communication, and build accuracy.
look_fors: Student is actively contributing to the team.
Student is using role language and tool names.
Student can explain what they did when asked.
conference_prompts: What did you do today in your role?
What does your planter still need?
checkpoint_sheet.release_rule: Build checklist must be complete before students move to Week 6 planting activities.

days.carryover_note: Planting Record and Care Schedule carry forward to Week 7 observations.

content.subtitle: Today we plant seeds and start the care cycle. Record everything you do.

rows: seed — the small unit we plant to grow a new plant
soil — the growing material that holds moisture and nutrients
spacing — how far apart the seeds are placed
depth — how deep below the surface the seed is planted
germinate — when a seed begins to sprout and grow

steps: Fill the planter with soil to about 3 cm below the top edge.
Use your finger or a pencil to make a small hole for each seed.
Place one seed in each hole at the correct depth.
Gently cover each seed with soil.
Water lightly — the soil should be damp, not soaking.
Label the planter with your team name and the seed type.

content.scenario: Now that we have planted, we need a plan for caring for the plants every week.
prompts: Who checks the soil?
Who waters when needed?
Who records growth each week?
Today we planted ___ seeds.
The seeds are in the ___ (planter box / starter cups / trays).
Our first care task is ___ .

task_sheet.title: Week 6 Task Sheet — Planting Record and Care Schedule
instructions: Complete the Planting Record as you plant today.
Fill in the Care Schedule for the coming weeks.
Keep this sheet — you will need it every week.

tasks.label: Planting Record
tasks.prompt:
Fill in the record while you plant.

Date planted: ___________________
Seed type: ___________________
Number of seeds: ___________________
Where planted: ___________________
Depth (how deep): ___________________
Who planted: ___________________
First observation: ___________________

tasks.label: Care Schedule
tasks.prompt:
Set up your weekly care routine. Fill in who will do each job.

Check soil: ___________________
Water when needed: ___________________
Look for growth: ___________________
Record what we see: ___________________
Clean the area: ___________________

render_hints.help: Level B: circle one name from your team for each job.

embedded_supports: Level A: draw what you planted instead of writing. Teacher scribes the labels.
Sentence frame: Today we planted ___ seeds. We will check them every ___ .
success_criteria: My Planting Record has a date, seed type, and location.
My Care Schedule shows at least two jobs assigned.
I can say what we planted and what comes next.

exit_ticket.title: Week 6 Exit Check
exit_ticket.prompt: Today we planted ___ seeds. We will take care of them by ___ . Next week we will check for ___ .
success_criteria: I named what we planted.
I described one care task.

days.carryover_note: Growth Log 1 data carries forward to Week 8 and Week 9.

content.subtitle: This week: observe your plant, record what you see, and send the client an update.

rows: Look before you measure. Notice colour, shape, and any growth.
Measure from the base of the soil to the top of the tallest stem.
Write exactly what you see — even 'nothing yet' is a useful observation.

content.task: Before writing in your Growth Log, answer these observation questions.
prompts: Is there any visible growth above the soil?
What does the soil look like — dry, damp, or soaking?
Has anything changed since planting day?

content.scenario: The client wants to know how the project is going. Write a short update using these sentence frames.
prompts: This week our plant is ___ .
It is ___ cm tall.
We noticed ___ .
Next we will ___ .
The most interesting thing we observed was ___ .
Our plant is ___ (growing well / just starting / not visible yet).
We think next week it will ___ .

graphic_organizer.title: Growth Log 1 — Week 7
graphic_organizer.prompt: Observe your plant carefully each week. Fill in one row for each observation. If nothing has grown yet, write 'no growth' and record care conditions instead.
success_criteria: I recorded the date and height (or 'no growth yet').
I noted whether the plant looks healthy.
I checked the care conditions.

discussion_prep_sheet.title: Weekly Client Update Card — Week 7
discussion_prep_sheet.discussion_prompt: What does the client need to know about our planter project this week?
success_criteria: I described the current state of the plant.
I included at least one observation detail.
I have a question or next step ready.

days.day_label: Week 8: Flex — Growth Check and Troubleshooting
days.carryover_note: Flex week. Growth Log 2 continues the data record from Week 7.

content.subtitle: This week: continue the growth log, solve any problems, and review care. Your teacher will tell you today's focus.

rows: Option A: Continue the growth log and review care conditions.
Option B: Use the Problem and Solution Sheet if growth is stalled or plants look unhealthy.
Option C: Begin comparing growth data if you have two or more log entries.

slides.title: What to do if nothing has grown

content.scenario: If nothing has sprouted yet, that is useful information. It means we need to look at the care conditions.
prompts: Is the soil staying damp?
Is the planter getting enough light?
Were the seeds planted at the right depth?

rows: germinate — when the seed begins to sprout
dormant — not growing yet, but not dead
overwatered — too much water can harm roots
underwatered — too little water causes wilting
sprout — the first visible growth above the soil

prompts: Our plant is ___ (growing / not yet growing / struggling).
One thing we are going to change or fix is ___ .
By Week 9, we expect our plant to ___ .

task_sheet.title: Week 8 Task Sheet — Problem and Solution
instructions: If your plant is growing well, skip Part 1 and go straight to Part 2.
If your plant is not growing or looks unhealthy, complete Part 1 first.
Complete Part 2 regardless.

tasks.label: Part 1: Problem and Solution (if needed)
tasks.prompt:
The problem I noticed is: ___________________

I think the problem is happening because: ___________________

I think we need to: ___________________

Our fix is: ___________________

render_hints.help: Problem examples: not enough water, not enough light, seeds too deep, soil too dry.

tasks.label: Part 2: Care Review Checklist
tasks.prompt:
Check your care routine.

☐ Soil has been checked at least twice this week.
☐ Plant was watered when soil was dry.
☐ Planter is in a location with enough light.
☐ Growth log has an entry this week.
☐ Team members know their care roles.
☐ Area around the planter is clean.

embedded_supports: Sentence frame: The problem is ___ . We think we need to ___ .
Level A: circle the problem from pictures shown by your teacher.
If nothing has grown yet, write 'no growth yet' — this is still a valid observation.
success_criteria: I identified any problems with my plant.
I completed the care review checklist.
I know what to do differently next week.

graphic_organizer.title: Growth Log 2 — Week 8
graphic_organizer.prompt: Continue recording growth from Week 7. Add new observations this week. If growth has started since last week, record the change.
success_criteria: I added at least one new row to the growth log.
I can compare this week's observation to last week's.

days.carryover_note: Recommendation draft and data table carry forward to Week 10 final report.

content.subtitle: This week: use your growth data to make a simple recommendation to the client.

rows: Look at your Growth Log. Find the tallest measurement you recorded.
Compare your first observation to your most recent one.
Use the numbers to describe what changed.

content.scenario: A recommendation is a short, clear statement about what worked and what the client should do.
prompts: Our team recommends ___ because ___ .
The best part of our plan was ___ .
If we did this again, we would ___ .

content.task: Compare what our plant looked like at the start and what it looks like now.

prompts: My recommendation says ___ .
The evidence I am using is ___ .
The most important thing the client needs to know is ___ .

graphic_organizer.title: Growth Data Table — Week 9
graphic_organizer.prompt: Copy your height measurements from your Growth Log into this table. Fill in all the weeks you recorded. Then circle the biggest number. This is your data for the recommendation.
success_criteria: I have at least two rows of data.
I circled the tallest measurement.
I can see whether the plant grew over time.

task_sheet.title: Week 9 Task Sheet — Recommendation Draft
instructions: Use your data table and Growth Log to complete this recommendation draft.
Your recommendation must include at least one number from your data.
This is a draft — you will use it in Week 10 to prepare your final report.

tasks.label: Data Summary
tasks.prompt:
Fill in these summary statements.

The tallest our plant grew was: ___ cm (Week ___)
Compared to when we planted, the plant is now ___ cm ___ (taller / shorter / the same).
The biggest change happened between Week ___ and Week ___ .

render_hints.help: Level B: just fill in the blanks. Level C: add one sentence explaining why you think the change happened.

tasks.label: Recommendation Draft
tasks.prompt:
Write your recommendation to the client. Use at least one number from your data.

Our team recommends: ___________________
Because: ___________________
The evidence shows: ___________________
For next time, we suggest: ___________________

render_hints.help: Sentence frame: Our team recommends ___ because our data shows ___.

embedded_supports: Level A: speak your recommendation to your teacher. Teacher scribes with you.
Sentence frames: Our plant grew from ___ cm to ___ cm. This shows that ___ .
Useful words: grew, increased, stayed the same, improved, changed, recommends
success_criteria: My data summary includes at least one number.
My recommendation draft includes a 'because' statement.
I am ready to turn this into my final report.

days.carryover_note: Final evidence week. Oral report and Final Recommendation Card are the closing evidence.

content.subtitle: Today we present our work to the client. Use your cue card and speak clearly.

rows: Stand still, make eye contact, and speak at a pace the listener can follow.
Use your cue card — it keeps you on track and shows you prepared.
End with your recommendation — that is the most important part.

steps: Start with the project: 'Our team built a planter box for ___.'
Describe the planting: 'We planted ___ seeds.'
Share the growth: 'Our plant grew to ___ cm.'
Make the recommendation: 'We recommend ___ because ___.'
Finish: 'Thank you for the job.'

content.scenario: Practice your report with a partner before presenting to the teacher or class.
prompts: Can your partner follow your report?
Did you include a number from your data?
Did your recommendation make sense?
I helped my team ___ .
The part I am most proud of is ___ .
Next time I would ___ .

discussion_prep_sheet.title: Oral Presentation Cue Card — Week 10
discussion_prep_sheet.discussion_prompt: Present your team's planter project to the client in under one minute.
success_criteria: I described what we built and planted.
I included at least one piece of data.
I made a clear recommendation with a reason.

final_response_sheet.title: Final Recommendation Card — Week 10
final_response_sheet.prompt: Write your team's final recommendation to the client. Use your data, your Recommendation Draft from Week 9, and your Oral Cue Card to help you. This is your official record of the project.
planning_reminders: Include your team name and the planter you built.
Include at least one measurement or piece of data.
Explain why you are making this recommendation.
Keep it short and clear — this is a professional report.

frame_strip: We planted ___ seeds and our plant grew to ___ cm.
Based on our results, we recommend ___ because ___.
If we did this again, we would ___.

success_criteria: I named the project and the client.
I included at least one number from my data.
My recommendation includes a 'because' statement.
I am proud of this as my final communication to the client.

checkpoint_sheet.title: Week 10 Teacher Conference — Final Report
checkpoint_sheet.checkpoint_focus: Observe oral presentation and review final recommendation card.
look_fors: Student can present a 30-second oral report clearly.
Report includes a recommendation with a reason.
Final Recommendation Card is complete with at least one data reference.
Student can speak to what their team accomplished.
conference_prompts: Tell me what your team built and planted.
What would you recommend to the client and why?
checkpoint_sheet.release_rule: Final Recommendation Card is the declared final evidence location. Oral report and self-check are supporting evidence.
